message.content: You are reviewing a memo for an experienced engineer who asked specifically for PLAIN, SIMPLE language and to be EDUCATED about a process. Read the memo at /Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster/Memos/memo-20260605-ultracode-replan-process.md. Judge it purely as teaching: Is each idea explained before it is used? Is there unexplained jargon? Does it explain the WHY behind each design choice (fan-out, structured output, adversarial verify, pipeline-not-barrier, human-kept judgment), not just the what? Is anything too abstract to picture? Is the length right, or does it sag? Flag concrete spots where a smart but busy reader would stumble, and give a specific rewrite for each. Do NOT check facts (another reviewer owns that); focus only on clarity and teaching value.

attachment.content:
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- rbk-wrangle: Muster, then roster RBK and JJK heats as two space-padded plain-text blocks
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll read the memo and evaluate it purely as teaching material.

content.content:
1	# Memo — How ultracode revised the Lode remainder plan (₢BHAAB)
2	
3	*2026-06-05. Written to educate the operator on the multi-agent process that produced the ₢BHAAB replan recommendations and the 10-pace slate. Plain-language walkthrough, not a spec.*
4	
5	## What this is about
6	
7	On 2026-06-05 the operator asked for recommendations on four open planning calls for heat ₣BH (the Lode universal-capture remainder), then said "slate now." Instead of answering those four calls from memory or from the paddock alone, the work ran an **ultracode workflow** — a script that fanned the question out across ten subagents — to ground every recommendation in the *actual landed code* before any pace was written. This memo explains what that process was, why it was shaped the way it was, what it caught, and what it cost.
8	
9	## What "ultracode" changed
10	
11	Ultracode is a session mode. With it on, the default for any substantial task flips from "one agent (me) does it inline" to "author a workflow that decomposes the task across many subagents, and accept high token cost in exchange for the most thorough, correct answer." The lever is the **Workflow tool**: a small JavaScript program that spawns subagents, collects their results, and lets me orchestrate them deterministically (loops, fan-out, verify stages) rather than improvising agent-by-agent.
12	
13	The key mental shift: I stop being the one who reads all the files, and become the one who *designs the investigation* and *makes the judgment* on what comes back.
14	
15	## The shape of the run
16	
17	The four open calls each touched a *different* part of the codebase, so the natural move was to give each its own reader. The workflow ran five investigators in parallel, and each one's findings were immediately handed to an independent skeptic to double-check:
18	
19	```
20	            INVESTIGATE                 VERIFY
21	  augur      ──reader──►  findings  ──►  skeptic re-reads the cited files
22	  scaffold   ──reader──►  findings  ──►  skeptic re-reads the cited files
23	  vertical   ──reader──►  findings  ──►  skeptic re-reads the cited files
24	  cutover    ──reader──►  findings  ──►  skeptic re-reads the cited files
25	  provdocs   ──reader──►  findings  ──►  skeptic re-reads the cited files
26	```
27	
28	Ten agents total: five to find the facts, five to attack them. I then read all ten results and wrote the recommendations myself.
29	
30	## The five design choices, and why each one
31	
32	**1. Fan out by question, not by file.** The four calls map to disjoint subsystems — the divine/augur read verbs, the per-kind capture bodies, the cutover chokepoints, the provenance/docs surface. One agent holding all of that would read shallowly across everything. Five agents each read one area deeply. Coverage comes from breadth of agents, not depth of any single context.
33	
34	**2. Force structured answers.** Each investigator was required to return a fixed shape: a list of *claims*, each with *evidence* (a file-and-line citation) and a *confidence* level, plus a single *recommendation input*. This does two things — it makes the agent show its work (no claim without a citation), and it lets me combine ten results mechanically instead of parsing ten free-text essays.
35	
36	**3. Verify adversarially.** Recommendations that decide a ten-pace plan must rest on facts that are actually true. So every investigator's findings went to a second agent whose only job was to *re-read the cited files and try to break the claims*. The skeptic didn't trust the summary; it opened the files itself. This is the single most valuable part of the run (see below).
37	
38	**4. Pipeline, don't barrier.** The five areas have no dependency on each other, so there was no reason to wait for all five investigations to finish before starting any verification. Each area's skeptic started the moment that area's investigation landed. The whole job finished at the speed of the slowest single chain, not the sum of the stages.
39	
40	**5. Keep the judgment.** The agents produced *facts*; I produced the *recommendation*. Ultracode amplified the research, not the decision. The "group tool+reliquary, split podvm, slate the bole cutover early" calls were mine, made after reading what the agents found. That division — machines gather, human-in-the-loop judges — is deliberate. The recommendation is a collaboration artifact, and it stayed in my voice.
41	
42	## What the verify stage actually caught
43	
44	The skeptics earned their place. Real corrections they surfaced:
45	
46	- **An overstatement.** One investigator said the bole capture emits a "byte-equivalent coordinate" to the old enshrine path. The skeptic re-read both and found only the *fingerprint token* is identical; the full locator differs (`rbi_es/<fp>` vs `rbi_ld/<stamp>:<fp>`). That correction changed how I wrote the bole-cutover docket — it now says "read the touchmark from the capture-file," because the Lode path can't be synthesized from origin+digest the way the old one could.
47	- **Over-claimed facts.** An investigator assigned trust grades to all five kinds at "high confidence." The skeptic checked the spec and found only two kinds are actually pinned there; the rest were the investigator's reasonable guess dressed as fact. The docket now flags those as open decisions.
48	- **Citation slips.** Several "file:line" references pointed at the right content in the wrong file, or a comment instead of real code. Minor, but exactly the kind of thing that rots a plan if it's trusted blindly.
49	
50	**And one false positive worth dwelling on.** A skeptic flagged the per-kind verb names (`fetter`, `conclave`, `underpin`, `immure`) as *fabricated* — invented by the investigator. They were not: they are the paddock's settled vocabulary. The skeptic flagged them only because the subagents never received the paddock; they could see the code but not the design document. I caught this because I *did* have the paddock, and overrode the "correction."
51	
52	The lesson there is the important one: **a verifier is only as good as the context it's given.** The agents could check code-against-code perfectly, but they couldn't check code-against-a-design-doc they never saw. The human in the loop exists precisely to reconcile agent output against knowledge the agents didn't have. Ultracode does not remove that seat; it makes filling it more important.
53	
54	## What it cost
55	
56	Ten agents, ~937,000 subagent tokens, ~200 tool calls, about eight minutes of wall-clock. That is a lot of tokens to answer four planning questions. The trade is deliberate: token cost bought *coverage* (four subsystems read in depth at once) and *confidence* (every load-bearing fact independently re-checked). For a one-off answer that would be wasteful; for the decision that shapes the next ten paces of a heat, it was worth it.
57	
58	## When this is the right tool, and when it isn't
59	
60	It fit here because the task was **wide** (four disjoint areas), **fact-heavy** (the right answer depended on the actual code, not on taste), and **consequential** (it set a multi-pace plan). Those three together justify the fan-out-and-verify shape.
61	
62	It would have been the wrong tool for a narrow lookup ("what does this one function return?"), for a pure judgment call with no facts to gather, or for anything where the knowledge already lived in one context. Notably, *this very memo* was a borderline case: the process being described lived in my context, not in the codebase, so the subagents could only review a draft I wrote — they couldn't have authored it from scratch.
63	
64	## A closing note
65	
66	This memo was itself put through a small ultracode review panel — independent agents checking it for factual accuracy against the workflow's own script and results, for plain-language clarity, and for anything missing — before being committed. The process described here was used to refine the description of itself.
67

content.text:
Now let me look at the workflow script and related materials to understand the subject matter well enough to judge whether the teaching lands. But my mandate is clarity and teaching value only, not fact-checking. Let me focus on reading the memo as a smart-but-busy reader would.

Let me check whether some terms used in the memo are jargon local to this codebase or explained inline. The memo uses terms like "Lode," "bole," "paddock," "docket," "touchmark," "enshrine," "reliquary," "podvm," "augur," "divine." I need to judge whether these would trip a reader. The target reader is "an experienced engineer" who asked to be "EDUCATED about a process" — the process being the ultracode multi-agent workflow, not the Lode domain. Let me assess each carefully.

I have enough to evaluate the memo as teaching. Let me produce my review.

issues.where: The whole-memo jargon load: "Lode universal-capture remainder", "the divine/augur read verbs", "the per-kind capture bodies", "the cutover chokepoints", "bole capture", "enshrine path", "rbi_es/<fp> vs rbi_ld/<stamp>:<fp>", "touchmark", "reliquary", "podvm", "fetter, conclave, underpin, immure".
issues.problem: The reader asked to be EDUCATED ABOUT A PROCESS (the multi-agent workflow). But the memo teaches the process while assuming fluency in the Lode domain it was applied to. A smart reader who knows the ultracode mechanics but not this codebase's vocabulary will stall repeatedly on domain nouns that are used but never explained. The teaching target (the process) gets blurred by an un-taught second subject (the domain). The reader cannot tell which words they are supposed to learn here and which they are assumed to already own.
issues.fix: Add one or two sentences early (right after the opening, before "The shape of the run") that say explicitly: "You don't need to know the Lode domain to follow this memo — wherever a domain word like 'bole' or 'reliquary' appears, read it as 'one of the subsystems being investigated.' What matters is the process around it." Then, at the few spots where a domain detail is actually load-bearing for the teaching point (the rbi_es/rbi_ld example), add a half-sentence gloss: "...the old path stored the image at one address and the new Lode path at a differently-shaped one, so the new address can't be reconstructed from the inputs the way the old one could."

issues.where: Section "The shape of the run": "The four open calls each touched a *different* part of the codebase" — but the diagram and the surrounding text list FIVE investigators (augur, scaffold, vertical, cutover, provdocs), and "Ten agents total: five to find the facts..."
issues.problem: Four-vs-five is a genuine comprehension snag for the careful reader the memo is written for. The memo says four open calls, then runs five investigators, and never explains why the count changed. A teaching memo must not leave the reader silently doing arithmetic that doesn't add up. (This is a clarity gap, not a fact error — the fact may be perfectly correct; it is simply unexplained.)
issues.fix: Add one sentence where the five investigators first appear: "(The four calls became five readers because one call — provenance and docs — spanned two surfaces wide enough to warrant its own agent each; the mapping from questions to agents is not always 1:1.)" Or whatever the real reason is. The point is to name the 4→5 jump out loud the moment it occurs.

issues.where: Section "What 'ultracode' changed": "The lever is the **Workflow tool**: a small JavaScript program that spawns subagents..." and later "author a workflow that decomposes the task across many subagents."
issues.problem: This is the one piece of genuinely new machinery the reader is being educated about, and it is introduced too fast to picture. "A small JavaScript program that spawns subagents" raises immediate questions the memo never answers: who writes the program (the answer — Claude writes it on the fly — is buried in the verb "author")? Is it run once, or is it a standing tool? What does a subagent actually receive and return? Without a concrete mental image of one subagent call, the rest of the architecture is abstract.
issues.fix: Add two or three sentences making one subagent call concrete: "Concretely: I write a short script. It loops, and on each pass it launches a fresh Claude instance — a subagent — handing it a focused prompt ("read these files, answer this one question, return your findings in this exact shape"). That subagent has its own clean context, does its reading, returns a structured result, and disappears. The script collects those results for me. I author this script per-task; it is not a fixed pipeline." This grounds every later mention of "agent" in something the reader can see.

issues.where: Design choice 4: "**Pipeline, don't barrier.** The five areas have no dependency on each other, so there was no reason to wait for all five investigations to finish before starting any verification... The whole job finished at the speed of the slowest single chain, not the sum of the stages."
issues.problem: "Barrier" is used as the contrast term but never defined — it is concurrency jargon (a barrier = a sync point where all parallel work must finish before anyone proceeds). The reader is asked to understand the WHY of pipeline-not-barrier without being told what a barrier is. The payoff sentence ("speed of the slowest single chain, not the sum of the stages") is correct but compressed; a busy reader has to stop and verify the timing claim themselves.
issues.fix: Define the contrast in plain words: "The obvious way (a 'barrier') is two phases: run all five investigations, wait for the last one, THEN start verifying. That wastes time — verification of the area that finished first sits idle waiting for the slowest investigation. Instead, each area's skeptic starts the instant that area's investigation lands. The areas run as five independent investigate-then-verify chains, side by side, so total time is the longest single chain rather than (all investigations) + (all verifications)."

issues.where: Design choice 5 and the false-positive section both carry the human-judgment point: "machines gather, human-in-the-loop judges" and "The human in the loop exists precisely to reconcile agent output against knowledge the agents didn't have."
issues.problem: These are the two strongest teaching moments in the memo and they make essentially the same point in two places, ~12 lines apart, without the second acknowledging the first. The reader gets the abstract version (design choice 5) and then the concrete version (the paddock false-positive) as if they were unrelated. The memo doesn't connect them, so the concrete story doesn't land as the proof of the earlier principle — it reads as a second, separate observation. The teaching would be far stronger if the abstract claim were stated once and then explicitly cashed out by the story.
issues.fix: Make the false-positive story the payoff of design choice 5 rather than a free-floating lesson. In the verify section, open the false-positive paragraph with an explicit callback: "This is design choice 5 ("keep the judgment") earning its keep — here is exactly the kind of thing it exists to catch:" Then keep the story. That turns two restatements into one principle-plus-evidence, which is how the reader will actually remember it.

issues.where: Design choice 2: "a single *recommendation input*" and design choice 1: "the divine/augur read verbs, the per-kind capture bodies, the cutover chokepoints, the provenance/docs surface."
issues.problem: "Recommendation input" is an undefined coinage at the moment of use — the reader can guess it means "the agent's suggested takeaway for the final decision," but a teaching memo shouldn't make the reader guess a term it just invented. Separately, the parenthetical list in choice 1 is the densest domain-jargon clause in the memo and arrives right where the reader is trying to grasp the general principle ("fan out by question, not by file"), competing with it for attention.
issues.fix: Gloss the coinage inline: "a single *recommendation input* — the agent's own one-line suggestion for what the final call should be." And in choice 1, demote the jargon list to an aside or replace it with the count: "The four calls map to disjoint subsystems — read verbs, capture bodies, cutover points, provenance/docs. One agent holding all four would read shallowly across everything; four (here, five) agents each read one area deeply." Let the principle breathe without the full domain vocabulary riding on top of it.

issues.where: Overall length and the final two sections ("When this is the right tool..." and "A closing note").
issues.problem: The memo's teaching core is the design-choices section and the verify-caught section — those are tight and earn their length. The tail sags slightly: "When this is the right tool" is good and belongs, but "A closing note" (the memo was itself reviewed by ultracode) is a cute reflexive flourish that adds no teaching and competes with the genuinely useful self-aware point already made at the end of the previous section ("this very memo was a borderline case"). The reader's attention is finite; the last thing they read should be the durable takeaway, not a meta-curio.
issues.fix: Either cut "A closing note" entirely, or fold its one substantive fact (independent agents checked this memo) into a single clause on the borderline-case sentence in the prior section: "...so the subagents could only review a draft I wrote — which is in fact how this memo was checked — rather than author it from scratch." End the memo on the when-to-use-it judgment, which is the highest-value parting thought.

input.verdict: As a teaching piece the memo is genuinely good — the five-design-choices spine is the right structure, and the verify-caught and false-positive sections are vivid and memorable. It is close to shippable but not yet there for its stated reader. The single most important change: the memo teaches the PROCESS while silently assuming fluency in the LODE DOMAIN it was run on, so a reader who knows multi-agent workflows but not this codebase stalls on un-taught nouns (bole, reliquary, touchmark, augur/divine, the rbi_es/rbi_ld example). Add one early sentence telling the reader they may read every domain word as \"one of the subsystems under investigation,\" and gloss the two or three spots where a domain detail is actually load-bearing. Fix the unexplained 4-calls-but-5-investigators jump in the same pass. Those two changes remove the only places a smart, busy reader actually stumbles; everything else is polish.

content.text:
I've completed the clarity-and-teaching review of the memo at `/Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster/Memos/memo-20260605-ultracode-replan-process.md` and returned it via StructuredOutput.

Summary of what I found: the memo's teaching spine (five design choices, each with its WHY) is well-built, and the verify-caught and paddock-false-positive sections are vivid and land. The main weakness is that it teaches the *process* while silently assuming fluency in the *Lode domain* it was applied to — domain nouns (bole, reliquary, touchmark, augur/divine, rbi_es/rbi_ld) are used but never explained, which is where the stated reader will actually stall. The other concrete stumble is the unexplained jump from "four open calls" to "five investigators." I also flagged the too-fast introduction of the Workflow tool (needs one concrete subagent-call picture), the undefined concurrency term "barrier," the duplicated human-judgment point that should be fused into principle-plus-evidence, and a slightly sagging tail ("A closing note"). Each issue has a specific rewrite.
